codeSnapshot.label: gcn_cora.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch_geometric.nn import GCNConv
from torch_geometric.datasets import Planetoid

# --- Load Cora citation network ---
dataset = Planetoid(root='/tmp/Cora', name='Cora')
data    = dataset[0]
print(f"Nodes: {data.num_nodes}, Edges: {data.num_edges}")
print(f"Features: {data.num_features}, Classes: {dataset.num_classes}")
print(f"Train mask: {data.train_mask.sum()} nodes")

# --- 2-layer GCN ---
class GCN(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels, hidden, out_channels):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = GCNConv(in_channels, hidden)
        self.conv2 = GCNConv(hidden, out_channels)

    def forward(self, x, edge_index):
        x = F.relu(self.conv1(x, edge_index))   # H^(1) = ReLU(AH^(0)W^(0))
        x = F.dropout(x, p=0.5, training=self.training)
        return self.conv2(x, edge_index)         # H^(2) = AH^(1)W^(1)

device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
model  = GCN(dataset.num_features, 16, dataset.num_classes).to(device)
data   = data.to(device)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.01, weight_decay=5e-4)

for epoch in range(200):
    model.train()
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    out  = model(data.x, data.edge_index)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(out[data.train_mask], data.y[data.train_mask])
    loss.backward(); optimizer.step()

model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    pred = model(data.x, data.edge_index).argmax(dim=1)
    acc  = (pred[data.test_mask] == data.y[data.test_mask]).float().mean()
print(f"Test accuracy: {acc.item():.4f}")  # ~0.81

eli3: GCNs (Graph Convolutional Networks) apply convolution-like operations to graphs — data structures where items (nodes) are connected to each other (edges). Unlike images where each pixel has the same number of neighbours in the same positions, graph nodes can have any number of connections to any other nodes.

The core idea: each node updates its representation by averaging the features of all its neighbours (plus itself), then multiplying by a learned weight matrix. Do this for a few rounds, and each node's representation captures not just its own features, but information from its local neighbourhood.

GCNs are used for tasks like: predicting whether a protein will bind to a drug (nodes = atoms, edges = bonds), predicting citation categories for academic papers (nodes = papers, edges = citations), or detecting fraud in transaction networks (nodes = accounts, edges = transactions).

whenWhy.title: Node classification on citation and social networks
whenWhy.body: GCN was introduced for semi-supervised node classification where only a fraction of nodes are labeled. Kipf & Welling (2017) demonstrated this on citation networks (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed): nodes = papers, edges = citations, features = bag-of-words representations. With only 20 labeled examples per class, GCN achieves 81.5% accuracy on Cora — significantly better than feature-only MLP (57%) or label propagation (68%). The message passing mechanism allows information to flow from labeled to unlabeled nodes through the graph structure.

whenWhy.title: Molecular property prediction
whenWhy.body: GCNs and their variants (MPNN, GIN) are the standard approach for predicting molecular properties (solubility, toxicity, binding affinity) from molecular graphs. Nodes = atoms (with atomic number, charge, hybridisation as features), edges = bonds (with bond type, aromaticity). The GCN aggregates atom features with bonding structure, producing a molecular fingerprint for property prediction. This is faster and often more accurate than traditional cheminformatics fingerprints (Morgan algorithm) for learned property prediction.

whenWhy.title: Recommendation systems with user-item graphs
whenWhy.body: Large-scale recommendation systems model users and items as nodes in a bipartite graph with edges = interactions (clicks, purchases, ratings). GCN-based methods (PinSage, LightGCN) propagate user preferences through the interaction graph: a user's representation is informed by the items they interacted with, which are in turn informed by other users who interacted with them. Pinterest's PinSage processes a graph with 3B nodes and 18B edges using mini-batch GCN training with importance sampling.

deepDiveIntro: The GCN propagation rule (Kipf & Welling, 2016) updates each node's representation by taking a normalised weighted average of its own features and those of all its neighbours, then applying a learned linear transformation and a nonlinearity. Adding self-loops ensures each node includes its own previous representation in the average. The normalisation divides each neighbour's contribution by the square root of both nodes' degrees, so high-degree hubs do not dominate the aggregation. [1]

The spectral interpretation derives this rule from graph signal processing: graph convolution in the frequency domain can be approximated with a single-parameter first-order filter, which collapses to the same symmetric normalisation used in the spatial view. The spatial interpretation is more intuitive — each node simply gathers a normalised average of its neighbourhood — but both perspectives lead to identical computations. [2]

deepDive.heading: Symmetric Normalisation: Why It Matters
deepDive.body: Symmetric normalisation is more statistically justified: it's equivalent to graph normalised cuts and is the standard in spectral graph theory.

deepDive.heading: Over-smoothing: Depth Limitation of GCN
deepDive.body: A critical limitation: deep GCNs suffer over-smoothing — as the number of layers increases, node representations converge to the same value and become indistinguishable. Li et al. (2018) showed that after sufficiently many layers, all node representations converge to a single point proportional to the square root of each node's degree, regardless of the input features.

deepDive.heading: Semi-Supervised Learning with GCN
deepDive.body: GCN aggregates features across the graph, so unlabeled nodes influence the representations of labeled nodes and vice versa — the graph structure acts as a regulariser that propagates label information. This is a form of graph-based semi-supervised learning without explicit graph Laplacian regularisation (as in earlier methods like label propagation). Performance comparison (Cora, 140 labeled nodes): Logistic regression on features: 57.2%; DeepWalk: 67.2%; GCN: 81.5%.
deepDive.formula: L = −Σ_{i∈L} Σ_c Y_{ic} log(Z_{ic}),   Z = softmax( Â ReLU(Â X W^(0) ) W^(1) )

deepDive.heading: Mini-Batch Training and Neighbourhood Sampling
deepDive.body: Full-batch GCN computes the propagation on the entire graph at once — infeasible for large graphs (millions of nodes). Two main approaches for scalable training: (1) GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017) — for each target node, sample a fixed-size neighbourhood at each layer; compute the aggregation only over sampled neighbours; use mini-batch gradient descent on subgraphs. (2) Cluster-GCN (Chiang et al., 2019) — partition the graph into clusters (e.g., using METIS), sample entire clusters as mini-batches, computing gradients within-cluster only. The inter-cluster edges are discarded for the mini-batch (introducing approximation). In PyTorch Geometric, NeighborLoader implements (1): sample up to k neighbours per layer for each target node, building a sampled subgraph for each mini-batch. [2]

takeaways: GCN propagation: H^(l+1) = σ(D̃^{-1/2}ÃD̃^{-1/2}H^(l)W^(l)); symmetric normalisation prevents degree bias. [1]
Spectral derivation: first-order Chebyshev approximation of spectral graph convolution. [2]
Over-smoothing: deep GCNs converge to constant representations; use 2–3 layers + residual connections in practice. [2]
Semi-supervised: train on labeled nodes only; graph structure regularises by propagating label information. [1]
Mini-batch training: GraphSAGE neighbourhood sampling or Cluster-GCN for graphs too large for full-batch. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why does GCN add self-loops (Ã = A + I) before normalisation?
edgeCases.a: Without self-loops, a node's aggregation at layer l depends only on its neighbours' features from layer l-1 — the node's own features are not directly included. This means a node can receive information from neighbours but cannot directly retain its own identity. Adding a self-loop (Ã = A + I) includes the node itself in its own neighbourhood, so the aggregated representation is: h_v^{(l+1)} = σ(W^{(l)} · Σ_{u ∈ N(v) ∪ {v}} h_u^{(l)/normalisation}). This allows each node to retain some of its own representation while incorporating neighbourhood information. Without self-loops, GCN would need a separate residual connection to preserve node features — adding the self-loop is the simpler equivalent.

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between GCN and the simpler mean aggregation?
edgeCases.a: Simple mean aggregation: h_v^{(l+1)} = σ(W^{(l)} · mean_{u ∈ N(v) ∪ {v}}(h_u^{(l)})). GCN uses symmetric normalisation: 1/√(d_v+1)·√(d_u+1) per edge, not 1/(d_v+1). The difference: (1) simple mean treats all neighbours equally regardless of their degrees — a hub and a leaf contribute equally to the average. Symmetric normalisation down-weights contributions from high-degree nodes (hubs), reducing their influence on local representations. (2) Spectral motivation: symmetric normalisation is derived from spectral graph theory and corresponds to the random walk normalised Laplacian, with cleaner convergence properties. In practice, the difference in accuracy is modest; GraphSAGE with mean aggregation sometimes outperforms GCN on certain benchmarks.

sources.label: Kipf & Welling (2016) — Semi-Supervised Classification with Graph Convolutional Networks, ICLR 2017 arXiv:1609.02907
Wu et al. (2019) — Simplifying Graph Convolutional Networks, ICML 2019 arXiv:1902.07153
